g.data-narration: Data is the foundation of all computational processes. Understanding data types helps design efficient information systems.
g.text: • Data type determines storage mechanism and processing technique
• Proper classification enables efficient querying and analysis
• Different sources require different collection and validation methods

g.data-narration: There are three main data types: Structured (tables), Semi-Structured (tags), and Unstructured (no format).
Structured data follows a rigid schema with rows and columns. It's stored in relational databases and queried using SQL.
g.text: • Rigid predefined schema | Rows & Columns | SQL queries
• Examples: Relational databases, spreadsheets, transaction systems

g.data-narration: Semi-structured data has organizational tags but no rigid schema. Common formats include JSON, XML, and HTML documents.
g.text: • Flexible schema with tags/markers | Hierarchical structure
• Examples: JSON, XML, HTML, Email messages

g.data-narration: Unstructured data has no predefined format and accounts for 80% of enterprise data. Includes text, images, audio, and video.
g.text: • No predefined format | ~80% of enterprise data | Big Data challenge
• Examples: Text, Images, Video, Audio, Social Media posts

g.data-narration: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable. Qualitative data describes qualities that cannot be measured numerically.
Data sources are categorized as Internal (within organization) and External (outside organization).
g.text: Within organization | ERP, CRM, Transaction systems
Outside organization | Government, Market research, Social media

g.data-narration: Internal data comes from within the organization - operational systems, transactions, and employee records. It's reliable and well-documented.
g.text: • Generated within the organization | Structured and well-documented
• Direct control over data quality and consistency

g.data-narration: External data comes from outside the organization - government agencies, market research, and purchased third-party data.
g.text: • Obtained outside organization | Requires validation and cleaning
• Provides broader context: market trends, competitor data, regulations

g.data-narration: Primary data is collected fresh for specific purposes. Secondary data is reused from existing sources.
g.text: Reused existing data | Reports, Publications, Statistics

g.data-narration: Generated data is produced as a byproduct of system operations - logs, sensor readings, clickstreams, and GPS coordinates.
g.text: • Byproduct of operations | Growing rapidly with IoT and digital platforms
• Examples: System logs, Clickstream, Sensor data, GPS, Social media activity

g.data-narration: E-commerce uses all data types: structured (databases), semi-structured (JSON reviews), and unstructured (product images).
g.text: • Semi-Structured: Reviews as JSON, Product catalogs (NoSQL)
• Unstructured: Product images, Videos, Customer chat logs

g.data-narration: This table summarizes data types with their storage technologies: SQL for structured, NoSQL for semi-structured, and Big Data for unstructured.
Remember: Structured data uses SQL, semi-structured uses NoSQL, and unstructured requires big data technologies. Internal sources are reliable; external provide context.
g.text: ✓ 3 Data Types: Structured (tables), Semi-Structured (tags), Unstructured (no format)
✓ ~80% of enterprise data is Unstructured - drives Big Data initiatives

g.data-narration: For exams: Remember that JSON and XML are semi-structured, NOT unstructured. Structured data uses SQL, and 80% of enterprise data is unstructured.
g.text: • JSON/XML = Semi-Structured (has tags but no rigid schema)
• External ≠ Secondary (external can be primary if collected directly)
